title: Color and Hue Jitter
concept: Hue and saturation jitter randomly nudge a training image's colors around the color wheel and how vivid they look, teaching a CNN to recognize objects despite camera white-balance and lighting color casts.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Brightness jitter shifts overall lightness, and contrast jitter stretches the light-dark spread. What do both leave alone?

options.text: The hue and saturation of the colors — this lesson's topic
The position of every object in the frame
Nothing — brightness and contrast jitter already change everything about an image

exercises.explain: Right — brightness and contrast only touch lightness. Now let's jitter the actual colors.

cards.title: One red shirt, three cameras
cards.body: Photograph the same red shirt with a phone camera indoors under warm bulbs, then under cool office fluorescents, then outdoors in daylight. The shirt is identical, but one photo looks orange-red, one looks slightly green-tinted, and one looks true red.

cards.title: Hue jitter and saturation jitter
cards.body: Hue jitter randomly rotates every color in an image a little around the color wheel (red toward orange, blue toward purple, and so on). Saturation jitter randomly boosts or mutes how vivid or washed-out the colors look, without changing brightness or position.

cards.title: Nudging the color-temperature and vividness sliders
cards.body: Think of a photo-editing app with a color-temperature slider and a vividness slider. Hue jitter is like randomly nudging the color-temperature slider a little each time; saturation jitter is like randomly nudging the vividness slider. Small random nudges, not wild swings.

cards.title: One apple, three light sources
cards.body: A fruit classifier's training photo of an apple gets copied and jittered: one copy looks slightly warmer as if under incandescent light, one looks slightly cooler as if under fluorescent light, one looks a touch more vivid. The model learns "apple" survives all of these color casts.

cards.title: Jitter is deliberately kept small
cards.body: It's tempting to worry that hue jitter could turn a yellow banana green, breaking the very thing that makes it recognizable. That's exactly why real hue and saturation jitter use a small, bounded random range — enough to cover camera and lighting variation, not enough to repaint an object into a different-looking one.

left.text:
Rotates colors around the wheel
Simulates white-balance shifts
Brightness and shape stay the same

right.text:
Boosts or mutes color vividness
Simulates washed-out vs. vivid cameras
Hue and shape stay the same

exercises.prompt: What does hue jitter do to a training image?

options.text: Randomly rotates every color a little around the color wheel
Randomly shifts the whole image lighter or darker

exercises.explain: Right — hue jitter nudges colors around the color wheel, like red drifting slightly toward orange.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Saturation jitter changes how vivid colors look without moving any pixels or changing the overall brightness.
exercises.explain: True. Saturation jitter only boosts or mutes color vividness; positions and average brightness stay the same.

exercises.sentence: Color jitter is kept within a small, realistic range so an object's ___ stays recognizable.
exercises.explain: Too much hue or saturation jitter could repaint an object's colors enough to change what it looks like — a small range avoids that.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is the clearest example of hue jitter applied to a photo of an orange?

options.text: The orange looks slightly more red-orange in one copy and slightly more yellow-orange in another, same shape, same spot in the frame
The orange gets rotated 45 degrees in the frame
The orange gets cropped so only half of it is visible

exercises.explain: Correct — a slight rotation around the color wheel, with nothing repositioned, is hue jitter.

exercises.prompt: A plant-identification app trained on photos from one phone model performs poorly on photos from other phones, which all render slightly different colors due to different camera white-balance algorithms. What's the most direct photometric fix?

options.text: Add hue and saturation jitter during training so the model sees the same plants under a realistic range of color casts and vividness levels
Add more geometric augmentation like flips and crops, since camera differences are a positional issue
Only train on photos from that one phone model to keep colors perfectly consistent
Apply extreme hue jitter that rotates colors all the way around the wheel to cover every possibility

exercises.explain: Exactly! Hue and saturation jitter, kept within a realistic range, directly target camera-to-camera color variation. Next: another photometric trick — deliberately injecting noise.
